records.ety: [OE. thinne, thenne, thunne, AS. þynne; akin to D. dun, G. dünn, OHG. dunni, Icel. þunnr, Sw. tunn, Dan. tynd, Gael. & Ir. tana, W. teneu, L. tenuis, Gr. <?/ (in comp.) stretched out, <?/ stretched, stretched out, long, Skr. tanu thin, slender; also to AS. <?/enian to extend, G. dehnen, Icel. <?/enja, Goth. <?/anjan (in comp.), L. tendere to stretch, tenere to hold, Gr. <?/ to stretch, Skr. tan. √51 & 237. Cf. Attenuate, Dance, Tempt, Tenable, Tend to move, Tenous, Thunder, Tone.]

defs: Having little thickness or extent from one surface to its opposite; as, a thin plate of metal; thin paper; a thin board; a thin covering.
Rare; not dense or thick; -- applied to fluids or soft mixtures; as, thin blood; thin broth; thin air.
quotes: In the day, when the air is more thin.
Satan, bowing low His gray dissimulation, disappeared, Into thin air diffused.

defs: Not close; not crowded; not filling the space; not having the individuals of which the thing is composed in a close or compact state; hence, not abundant; as, the trees of a forest are thin; the corn or grass is thin.
quotes: Ferrara is very large, but extremely thin of people.

defs: Not full or well grown; wanting in plumpness.
quotes: Seven thin ears . . . blasted with the east wind.

defs: Not stout; slim; slender; lean; gaunt; as, a person becomes thin by disease.
Wanting in body or volume; small; feeble; not full.
quotes: Thin, hollow sounds, and lamentable screams.

defs: Slight; small; slender; flimsy; wanting substance or depth or force; superficial; inadequate; not sufficient for a covering; as, a thin disguise.
quotes: My tale is done, for my wit is but thin.
notes: ☞ Thin is used in the formation of compounds which are mostly self-explaining; as, thin-faced, thin-lipped, thin-peopled, thin-shelled, and the like.

defs: Not thickly or closely; in a seattered state; as, seed sown thin.
quotes: Spain is thin sown of people.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Thinned (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Thinning.]

defs: To make thin (in any of the senses of the adjective).
To grow or become thin; -- used with some adverbs, as out, away, etc.; as, geological strata thin out, i. e., gradually diminish in thickness until they disappear.
